text: The surgeon mobilizes the splenic angle and ligates the inferior mesenteric vein and artery. Using a diatomy hook and swab, the total mesorectal excision begins posteriorly, assessing the tissue plane before proceeding anteriorly and laterally to ensure safe margins under the tumor.

After freeing the mesorectum, the surgeon staples the rectal tube below the tumor, potentially requiring multiple staple lines. The rectal stump remains at the bottom of the pelvis, where a new anastomosis between the colon and rectum will be performed. The specimen is extracted through a suprapubic incision.

The surgeon divides the mesenteric vessels at the predetermined level and assesses the bowel viability using indocyanine green dye. The video displays three views: a normal view, a black and white view, and an augmented reality view showing the dye in green and areas lacking vascularization in black. A clear cut-off is visible, confirming the bowel's viability for anastomosis. The surgeon performs a low situate anastomosis and reassesses the anastomosis and microvascularization using the three views on the left screen.

Following the injection of endocyanin IV, the surgeon observes the signal appearing first on the colonic side, highlighting the staple line in normal light. The rectal stem, which appears pale, is examined for microvascularization patterns visible externally, with microvessels along its length. A spatial rectoscope can be used for internal assessment if needed.
The final view shows the anastomosis in enhanced imaging after a low anterior resection, with a protective ileostomy and pelvic drainage visible.
